transcript.transcript: This is an interview about Julia Davis Park by Robert P. Jones from Boise, Idaho. You're Robert P. Jones, and this is Boise, Idaho on February 14, 2007. No, 15th. 15th. What did I say? 14th. That was yesterday. All right. On Valentine's Day. Valentine's Day. Well, thank you for writing back to us about our memory project. And We're just interested in knowing about your memories of the park. Doesn't have to be anything special, but just if you want to start off with some of your earliest memories and go forward, or however you'd like to do it. Probably easier to start at the beginning and work backwards. Well, the earliest memories I have are probably about 1932, Let's see, about 1932, 33. And We had our family picnics down there in the park. Generally on the 4th of July. Do they have fireworks there? I'll tell you, at that time, Boise had so many fireworks, there wasn't anything. They didn't have any organized fireworks. There was every kid in town and every family shot their own fireworks. The North End was really lit up probably a week before the 4th and a week after the 4th. And what do you remember about the park at that time? Was there a zoo? Well, they just had tables and fire pits. Yes, I think they did have outdoor prudies. But it was pretty primitive. But it was pretty primitive. The, let's see, at that time, you see MK that had that gravel pit, and there was a lake clear up to where the boathouse is now. And from there, clear over to the river. It's quite a sizable pond. And the lake was the water filled into the gravel pit? Right. Mm-hmm. Well, then the Boise City Canal, they had an overflow. And it's still in existence, I think. It goes from back Warm Springs down to comes in about where the Discovery Center is now. You're an engineer, so you know all about this. Oh. You see that pond, the gravel pond, was quite deep. It was probably at least 20, 25 feet deep. And then when the city got involved, they filled it in, and it became very shallow. Why do you think they did that? Probably for safety more than anything. I had heard somebody was drowned, but I don't been. I myself don't remember anybody drowned in there. And I don't want anything specific. But it could be a possibility. And you fished there? Yes. I lived up on North 11th. Yeah, 12th Street at that time, up on 1108 North 12th. And all the kids in that area would, on the weekend, would hike down from the north end down to Judy Davis and fish, spend the day. Would this be all boys or girls too? No, no girls. No girls? No, no girls. Uh-huh. So this is what you do on the weekend? You would do this on the weekend? Generally on Saturdays or holidays. And did you catch fish? Oh, yes. Did you sell them, or did you take them home? Well, the perch, we'd take the perch home. On the slews, we'd catch these carp. And what we'd do when we came through Chinatown, we'd trade them for firecrackers. Excuse me. So you're just telling me that when you came across the car up on the sluices, that you take them through Chinatown? Right. Chinatown was on Front Street and then Capitol Boulevard from Front almost up to Main Street on both sides of the street. What do you remember about it, Chinatown? Well, the Chinese people were very friendly and very generous, at least to the kids. I remember on Kepp Boulevard, they had two-story houses or apartments. And they always had banners and flags and things hanging from there. And inside their shops, everything was on the walls. And when they meant around the 4th of July, when they had fireworks, they just had, they'd just go crazy. Now, they'd have these tables there, and you could walk in. They had thousands of firecrackers, and sparklers, rockets. And I often wondered, as I got older, why they never had any explosions or fires or anything down there. But as far as I know, they never did. And as far as you knew, were they imported from China? Everything came from China. And They had a lot of tea. To sell? To sell. And then they had these lychee nuts. And at Christmas time, the merchants would give the kids the lychee nuts. The Chinese merchants would? The Chinese merchants would give you? Right, well, to the families that traded with them. Uh-huh. Right. Gave away the nuts. So you would take these fish down to the cart. Right. Well, they'd see us coming, and we'd negotiate. Do you remember how much you got paid? Well, we'd get probably four or five packages of firecrackers. Oh, you got paid in firecrackers? Oh, yeah. Oh, yeah. That was the big thing. And about how old were you, do you think, Approximately? Well, probably at that time, probably about ten years old. Uh-huh. So that would have been in... About 1935. Uh-huh. So you like to fish down there. What about in the summer? Do you go play there a lot in the summer? Well, We'd start going down along the river probably about the middle of April, first of May, until school started in September. But you mostly fished in the pond rather than in the river? Well, or in the sluice. We didn't fish very much in the river. The ponds had most of the fish. And did the rivers, was that before the dams? Did they get real low in the summer? Yes. Well, let's see. When I was a kid, the Diversion Dam was the first dam. And it was built, I think, about 1905, 1906. And then Arrow Rock was built in, I think, finished in about 1917. And then there wasn't any other dams on the river. The next one was Anderson Dam. And they started that just before World War II. Uh-huh. And then in about 19, probably about 1960, they started Lucky Dam. Lucky dam. Uh-huh. Did you work on those? You were, did you work on them? You were a civil engineer? No, I didn't work on those. Uh-huh. No. I watched them, me and Bill, but I didn't. Uh-huh. Uh-huh. So aside from fishing and picnicking, do you have any other things that you used to do at the park? Well, we used to go down there and play football. You played football? You know, just pick-up games. Is that when you were older? No, that was between 10 and 12 years old. See, the only other place that had any grass was up at the Greens Memorial Park up there on 6th Street. Memorial Park on 6th Street? Uh-huh. I guess I don't know where that is. Well, at the corner of 6th and 4th, just back at Cunningham Place, there's a park there. Oh, OK. And they call that the Greens. And that was an old park. What about when you were in, where did you go to junior high? Let's see. You went to St. Joseph's. I went to St. Joseph's. And then from St. Joseph's, I went to St. Joseph's to the eighth grade. And then I went to St. Teresa's from the eighth or ninth grade through the 12th. I graduated from St. Teresa's. So do you remember using the park as you got older? And I feel like in the same place. Well, during that, I mean, actually, the park was probably integrated in Almost all the families at one time or other, I know that, like the Eagles, they'd have big picnics down there. And the labor union, AFL and CIO, of course, they weren't combined then. The labor unions had the big picnics down there. And the Kiwanis, they always had their Fourth of July. Are you a Kiwanian? No. I like to go for breakfast. You like to eat breakfast. That's still going on. But like I said in that little letter, the biggest thing I remember, and it was really a big affair for Boise, old C.C. Anderson, he'd have his back to school parade and picnic. Every kid in the country and their families would go down there and play games. I have a watermelon seed. And Mr. Anderson didn't have any kids. Uh-huh. And he loved kids. And this is during the Depression. And when he'd come down there, he'd have his pockets just full of rolls of nickels. And all these different races and things, whoever won, he'd give them a roll of nickels. Oh. And this was a yearly event he did? Yes. Uh-huh. Well, I think it quit. I don't know if he did it after World War II. Uh-huh. Pre-World War II. Pre-World War II. Uh-huh. Sounds fun. Oh, it was. Uh-huh. Yeah. Of course, you know, Boise wasn't very big then. Just that Everybody that could walk or ride a bike or, at that time, I guess, drive a car, they went down to the park and had a picnic. And ate watermelon, it lasted all day long. Uh-huh. Yeah. Did you ever see the landing strip from the Julia Davis that was over by where Boise City is now? Where the airport, airfield? The airport, well, that's another thing. When we'd go down fishing, we'd go across the river and go over to South Boise. And the airport was, let's see, the runway, I think, was parallel to College Boulevard. And then they had hangers between the runway and College Boulevard. And there was a big beacon light on the corner. A beacon light on the corner of, there's a Beacon Street down there. Yeah, I think it was on the corner of College Boulevard. Isn't there a Beacon Street right there? There's a Beacon Street. I wonder if that's it. But I can't, like, see the beacon, there was a beacon out on the mountain home desert, out at Mayfield. There was a beacon there. And then the next beacon was there at Boise Airport. And then there was another beacon out here on Beacon Light Road north of Eagle. But I don't know whether, I mean, I'm sure they named Beacon Street after the beacon, but I can't remember if it was on the corner there. But it was on College Boulevard. I mean, not College. University Drive? You said College Boulevard. See, College Boulevard runs East and West. And Broadway goes North and South. So it'd be at the corner of Broadway and College Boulevard. Did you ever see the planes? Oh, oh yeah. We used to hang out there at the hangars. I knew all the pilots. Oh, really? Oh, yeah. You see, Boise wasn't very big then. It was not. They let us in the hangars. And the thing I remembered about being on those hangers at that time the wings and the rudders and the airy-wams were all fabric And they would take acetone and treat the fabric. And it was a pretty smelly place. And you know, it's a wonder. I imagine they did have fires, but I never seen any fires there. In the summertime, when they were fixing those winks and fuselages, it really was pretty smelly. You see, this wasn't too far after World War I, and everybody was crazy about airplanes and flying. And all the kids were making model airplanes. And one of our neighbors was Jerry Hannafin. His dad and the family owned the Hannafin Cigar Store. And Jerry Hanifin was very interested in airplanes. And he ended up as editor for Time magazine. Jerry Hannafin did? The editor of Time magazine. He was the Aviation and Travel Editor. Aviation and Travel, I didn't know that. Did you go to the park with him? Pardon? Did you go to the park? Was he one of your friends? Did you go to the park with him? Or was he a different age? Well, he was older. Oh. Yeah. But he was one of our, well, he was one of my idols, you might say. He was crazy about airplanes. And I used to go over. He made beautiful model airplanes. And they lived over on North 11th Street. I used to go over there and make up model airplanes with him. And his brother, Art, he became a Navy pilot. He flew for the Navy in World War II. But Jerry, he could, when he was in high school, he had one of his kidneys removed, so he never went through it. Was removed, and so he never went through. Uh-huh. Well, then, let's see. Did you, after you grew up, did you stay in Boise? Did you go in? No. The Marines, or the Navy. Navy. Navy. Right. Uh-huh. Well, no, I came back to Boise in 1947. And you've lived here ever since? And lived here ever since, yeah. So do you have some memories of the park after you came back, you know, to now? Well, We used to take our kids down to the park and the zoo. And the kids always liked to go on those boats, those paddle boats. Well, before they had the paddle boats, they had those little row boats. Did you ever ice skate at the park? No, my dad did, but. Your dad ice skated? Mm-hmm. No, I can't... We ice skated, but... We went down along the river. There was some other ponds along there where we used to stay. No, I don't think I ever stayed in on it. The other thing that was close to the park there, And K, their headquarters was where their first new multi-story building is. That used to be their hot plant. And that's where they passed all the asphalt from the city of Boise and Ada County. They mixed, they did what with it? They made it? The asphalt. I didn't hear what you said though. They made it or you used a different word. I didn't know what the word was. Oh, they mixed it. Mixed it, OK. So they mixed the asphalt there. See, they crushed the gravel right there. And then they, and that along on the east side of Park Boulevard, they had quarries in there. They crushed it. And they had the retort right there on Broadway. It's pretty smelly. And it was pretty smelly. And Harry Morrison, his office was on the west side of Broadway, just back in the inner mountain equipment. And you could walk down the street and you could always look in and see Harry sitting in his desk. He was a very striking gentleman. He had a head of white hair. He'd see you looking at him, he'd always wave at you. About what year was that? Well, that was in the 40s. Uh-huh. Well, we lived on North 11th Street, across the alley. Harry's brother lived there, Mr. Knutson. He was our neighbor for probably 15 years. And him and his brother actually started in K. Harry and his brother? Harry and his brother. I was trying to think, I was trying to think. I always called him Mr. Knudsen, But I can't remember what his first name was. It was Harry Morrison, and then this was his brother-in-law? No, his brother. But he had a different last name, Newtson? No, they were both Newtsons. Oh, I thought it was Harry Morrison. I mean, excuse me, I'm mixed up. Yeah, it was Harry Morrison and Mr. Knutson. Yeah, that was his, it was his partner. Uh-huh. Yes, uh-huh, yeah. Uh-huh. Yeah, I got mixed up, yeah. That's okay. Yeah. Yeah, Mr. Knutson, who lived on 11th Street, right next to... His partner. Right, right. Do you have any memories that your father told you? Because I know your father has given us a history. And he's been interviewed orally. But I wondered if there were any things Your father or mother taught you about the park when you were little, about what they did? Well. He said your father ice skated there. Yeah, he ice skated. He fished there. I know that they liked it. Oh, I know. Well, you know, Boise City, and I don't know whether I mentioned that or not, they had a band, I couldn't forget that band, and the band seemed to me like every Sunday afternoon they had a concert down there, and we'd go down to listen to music and have a picnic. And that was about in the, was that in the 30s? Oh, yes, uh-huh. And do you remember anything about when they played patriotic music? Oh, yeah, Sousa. I mean, it always started and ended with Sousa. Uh-huh. Do you remember if they had uniforms? They did. Yeah, they did have. Were they all men or were there men and women? I didn't see any women. There could have been, but they, No, they, they, I don't remember anything. Well that was something that you did. That sounds fun. Oh, it was. It was, right? And you know, another thing I see, the eagles. Eagles used to have picnics down there. And I remember that one. They had quite a little celebration when they put that monument thing down there at the park. Oh, the 10 Commandments? The 10 Commandments, yeah. So you remember that. And It was the Eagles, and that was for the movie, the Cecil B. DeMille movie. Was it when the Cecil B. DeMille movie was out? Right. Right. I think it came to Manly. And so you were there when it was? Right. You attended? Well, I just watched the monument. I wasn't an eagle, but. Watched the monument go in. Let's see. I think it was already in. And they had a picnic and a celebration. About it. I didn't seem sad about it. Uh-huh. Because a family, the Dwork family, their dad was a big eagle. And I was down there. Uh-huh. You're family. And I was family too.
